AI.motivation: LLMs often produce factually incorrect outputs, and existing uncertainty estimation methods do not adapt well to varying prompts, leading to inaccuracies in coverage.
AI.method: The authors extend conformal score transformation methods to LLMs, enabling prompt-adaptive calibration, which improves conditional coverage while retaining marginal coverage guarantees.
AI.result: The proposed approach outperformed existing methods in conditional coverage, providing better reliability and accuracy in long-form generation and multiple-choice question answering.
AI.conclusion: The adaptive conformal prediction strategy enhances the reliability of LLMs, supporting more accurate calibration and enabling selective filtering of unreliable outputs across diverse applications.

title: Artificial intelligence application in lymphoma diagnosis with Vision Transformer using weakly supervised training
summary: Vision transformers (ViT) have been shown to allow for more flexible feature detection and can outperform convolutional neural network (CNN) when pre-trained on sufficient data. Due to their promising feature detection capabilities, we deployed ViTs for morphological classification of anaplastic large cell lymphoma (ALCL) versus classic Hodgkin lymphoma (cHL). We had previously designed a ViT model which was trained on a small dataset of 1,200 image patches in fully supervised training. That model achieved a diagnostic accuracy of 100% and an F1 score of 1.0 on the independent test set. Since fully supervised training is not a practical method due to lack of expertise resources in both the training and testing phases, we conducted a recent study on a modified approach to training data (weakly supervised training) and show that labeling training image patch automatically at the slide level of each whole-slide-image is a more practical solution for clinical use of Vision Transformer. Our ViT model, trained on a larger dataset of 100,000 image patches, yields evaluation metrics with significant accuracy, F1 score, and area under the curve (AUC) at 91.85%, 0.92, and 0.98, respectively. These are respectable values that qualify this ViT model, with weakly supervised training, as a suitable tool for a deep learning module in clinical model development using automated image patch extraction.

AI.tldr: Vision transformers (ViT) outperform CNNs for lymphoma classification when pre-trained on enough data. A weakly supervised training approach increased practicality without sacrificing significant performance.
AI.motivation: The researchers aimed to address the limitations of lack of expertise resources in fully supervised training by developing a practical and effective ViT-based solution for lymphoma classification.
AI.method: ViT models were deployed for lymphoma classification. Initially trained with 1,200 image patches in a fully supervised setting, they later transitioned to weakly supervised training with automatic patch labeling using slide-level data from whole slide images on a larger dataset.
AI.result: The modified ViT model trained on 100,000 image patches achieved diagnostic metrics of 91.85% accuracy, an F1 score of 0.92, and an AUC of 0.98.
AI.conclusion: Weakly supervised ViT models demonstrate significant potential for practical and effective clinical applications in lymphoma classification through automated image patch extraction.

title: Composite Silhouette: A Subsampling-based Aggregation Strategy
summary: Determining the number of clusters is a central challenge in unsupervised learning, where ground-truth labels are unavailable. The Silhouette coefficient is a widely used internal validation metric for this task, yet its standard micro-averaged form tends to favor larger clusters under size imbalance. Macro-averaging mitigates this bias by weighting clusters equally, but may overemphasize noise from under-represented groups. We introduce Composite Silhouette, an internal criterion for cluster-count selection that aggregates evidence across repeated subsampled clusterings rather than relying on a single partition. For each subsample, micro- and macro-averaged Silhouette scores are combined through an adaptive convex weight determined by their normalized discrepancy and smoothed by a bounded nonlinearity; the final score is then obtained by averaging these subsample-level composites. We establish key properties of the criterion and derive finite-sample concentration guarantees for its subsampling estimate. Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets show that Composite Silhouette effectively reconciles the strengths of micro- and macro-averaging, yielding more accurate recovery of the ground-truth number of clusters.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Composite Silhouette metric for determining the number of clusters in unsupervised learning, addressing biases in existing metrics through adaptive weighting and subsampling.
AI.motivation: Existing metrics like micro-averaged Silhouette favor larger clusters, while macro-averaged Silhouette overemphasizes smaller clusters, leading to inaccurate cluster-count determination.
AI.method: Composite Silhouette combines micro- and macro-averaged Silhouette scores using an adaptive convex weight based on normalized discrepancy and utilizes subsampling to aggregate results across multiple clusterings.
AI.result: Experiments reveal Composite Silhouette can more accurately recover the true number of clusters compared to traditional metrics on both synthetic and real-world datasets.
AI.conclusion: Composite Silhouette reconciles biases in existing validation metrics and provides a robust approach for cluster-count selection in unsupervised learning.

title: DRG-Font: Dynamic Reference-Guided Few-shot Font Generation via Contrastive Style-Content Disentanglement
summary: Few-shot Font Generation aims to generate stylistically consistent glyphs from a few reference glyphs. However, capturing complex font styles from a few exemplars remains challenging, and the existing methods often struggle to retain discernible local characteristics in generated samples. This paper introduces DRG-Font, a contrastive font generation strategy that learns complex glyph attributes by decomposing style and content embedding spaces. For optimal style supervision, the proposed architecture incorporates a Reference Selection (RS) Module to dynamically select the best style reference from an available pool of candidates. The network learns to decompose glyph attributes into style and shape priors through a Multi-scale Style Head Block (MSHB) and a Multi-scale Content Head Block (MCHB). For style adaptation, a Multi-Fusion Upsampling Block (MFUB) produces the target glyph by combining the reference style prior and target content prior. The proposed method demonstrates significant improvements over state-of-the-art approaches across multiple visual and analytical benchmarks.

AI.tldr: The paper presents DRG-Font, a method to enhance font generation using a contrastive strategy to decompose glyph styles and contents.
AI.motivation: To address challenges in capturing and retaining complex font styles and local characteristics from a few exemplars in Few-shot Font Generation.
AI.method: A contrastive font generation architecture featuring a Reference Selection Module, Multi-scale Style and Content Head Blocks, and a Multi-Fusion Upsampling Block for style-content adaptation.
AI.result: Compared to state-of-the-art methods, DRG-Font achieves significant improvements on visual and analytical font generation benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Decomposing style and content spaces using the proposed architecture enhances font generation performance, especially in learning complex attributes from minimal references.

title: RPS: Information Elicitation with Reinforcement Prompt Selection
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have shown remarkable capabilities in dialogue generation and reasoning, yet their effectiveness in eliciting user-known but concealed information in open-ended conversations remains limited. In many interactive AI applications, such as personal assistants, tutoring systems, and legal or clinical support, users often withhold sensitive or uncertain information due to privacy concerns, ambiguity, or social hesitation. This makes it challenging for LLMs to gather complete and contextually relevant inputs. In this work, we define the problem of information elicitation in open-ended dialogue settings and propose Reinforcement Prompt Selection (RPS), a lightweight reinforcement learning framework that formulates prompt selection as a sequential decision-making problem. To analyze this problem in a controlled setting, we design a synthetic experiment, where a reinforcement learning agent outperforms a random query baseline, illustrating the potential of policy-based approaches for adaptive information elicitation. Building on this insight, RPS learns a policy over a pool of prompts to adaptively elicit concealed or incompletely expressed information from users through dialogue. We also introduce IELegal, a new benchmark dataset constructed from real legal case documents, which simulates dialogue-based information elicitation tasks aimed at uncovering case-relevant facts. In this setting, RPS outperforms static prompt baselines, demonstrating the effectiveness of adaptive prompt selection for eliciting critical information in LLM-driven dialogue systems.

AI.tldr: This paper focuses on enhancing the ability of Large Language Models (LLMs) to elicit concealed or incomplete user information during open-ended dialogues using a reinforcement learning framework called Reinforcement Prompt Selection (RPS).
AI.motivation: The motivation stems from the challenge LLMs face in interactive AI applications—users often withhold sensitive or uncertain information due to privacy concerns or hesitations, which limits the completeness of inputs during dialogues.
AI.method: A novel framework called Reinforcement Prompt Selection (RPS) is proposed, which uses reinforcement learning to adaptively choose dialogue prompts. Synthetic experiments and a newly created benchmark dataset, IELegal, were employed to validate the method.
AI.result: Through synthetic experiments and the new IELegal dataset, RPS outperformed static prompt baselines and random query methods, demonstrating its ability to adaptively elicit concealed information in dialogue systems.
AI.conclusion: RPS is effective in addressing the problem of adaptive information elicitation during open-ended dialogues, and its application holds promise for critical areas such as legal support, education, and personal assistants.

title: Dual-Enhancement Product Bundling: Bridging Interactive Graph and Large Language Model
summary: Product bundling boosts e-commerce revenue by recommending complementary item combinations. However, existing methods face two critical challenges: (1) collaborative filtering approaches struggle with cold-start items owing to dependency on historical interactions, and (2) LLMs lack inherent capability to model interactive graph directly. To bridge this gap, we propose a dual-enhancement method that integrates interactive graph learning and LLM-based semantic understanding for product bundling. Our method introduces a graph-to-text paradigm, which leverages a Dynamic Concept Binding Mechanism (DCBM) to translate graph structures into natural language prompts. The DCBM plays a critical role in aligning domain-specific entities with LLM tokenization, enabling effective comprehension of combinatorial constraints. Experiments on three benchmarks (POG, POG_dense, Steam) demonstrate 6.3%-26.5% improvements over state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a method combining interactive graph learning and LLM-based understanding for better product bundling recommendations, addressing cold-start challenges and graph modeling limitations. The approach achieves significant improvements over existing baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for product bundling struggle with cold-start items due to reliance on historical interactions and lack the ability to directly model graphs within LLMs.
AI.method: The method integrates interactive graph learning with LLM-based semantic understanding, utilizing a Dynamic Concept Binding Mechanism (DCBM) to convert graph structures into natural language prompts.
AI.result: Experiments conducted on three benchmarks (POG, POG_dense, Steam) demonstrated improvements ranging from 6.3% to 26.5% compared to state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method effectively addresses key challenges in product bundling by bridging graph learning and LLM capabilities, showcasing substantial performance gains in experimental tests.

title: UI-Copilot: Advancing Long-Horizon GUI Automation via Tool-Integrated Policy Optimization
summary: MLLM-based GUI agents have demonstrated strong capabilities in complex user interface interaction tasks. However, long-horizon scenarios remain challenging, as these agents are burdened with tasks beyond their intrinsic capabilities, suffering from memory degradation, progress confusion, and math hallucination. To address these challenges, we present UI-Copilot, a collaborative framework where the GUI agent focuses on task execution while a lightweight copilot provides on-demand assistance for memory retrieval and numerical computation. We introduce memory decoupling to separate persistent observations from transient execution context, and train the policy agent to selectively invoke the copilot as Retriever or Calculator based on task demands. To enable effective tool invocation learning, we propose Tool-Integrated Policy Optimization (TIPO), which separately optimizes tool selection through single-turn prediction and task execution through on-policy multi-turn rollouts. Experimental results show that UI-Copilot-7B achieves state-of-the-art performance on challenging MemGUI-Bench, outperforming strong 7B-scale GUI agents such as GUI-Owl-7B and UI-TARS-1.5-7B. Moreover, UI-Copilot-7B delivers a 17.1% absolute improvement on AndroidWorld over the base Qwen model, highlighting UI-Copilot's strong generalization to real-world GUI tasks.

AI.tldr: UI-Copilot enhances MLLM-based GUI agents in long-horizon tasks by introducing a lightweight copilot for memory retrieval and numerical computation.
AI.motivation: The study aims to address limitations of MLLM-based GUI agents, such as memory degradation and math hallucination, in long-horizon interaction scenarios.
AI.method: The authors propose UI-Copilot, which employs memory decoupling and tool-assisted learning methods like TIPO to enable better tool usage and task execution.
AI.result: UI-Copilot-7B outperforms other 7B-scale GUI agents on MemGUI-Bench and delivers significant improvements on AndroidWorld.
AI.conclusion: UI-Copilot demonstrates superior capabilities and generalization in managing long-horizon GUI tasks, setting a new benchmark in the field.

title: From Where Words Come: Efficient Regularization of Code Tokenizers Through Source Attribution
summary: Efficiency and safety of Large Language Models (LLMs), among other factors, rely on the quality of tokenization. A good tokenizer not only improves inference speed and language understanding but also provides extra defense against jailbreak attacks and lowers the risk of hallucinations. In this work, we investigate the efficiency of code tokenization, in particular from the perspective of data source diversity. We demonstrate that code tokenizers are prone to producing unused, and thus under-trained, tokens due to the imbalance in repository and language diversity in the training data, as well as the dominance of source-specific, repetitive tokens that are often unusable in future inference. By modifying the BPE objective and introducing merge skipping, we implement different techniques under the name Source-Attributed BPE (SA-BPE) to regularize BPE training and minimize overfitting, thereby substantially reducing the number of under-trained tokens while maintaining the same inference procedure as with regular BPE. This provides an effective tool suitable for production use.

AI.tldr: The paper focuses on improving tokenization in large language models, especially code tokenizers, by addressing issues of unused tokens and overfitting in existing methods.
AI.motivation: Token quality has a direct impact on efficiency and safety in language models by influencing speed, accuracy, and mitigating risks such as jailbreaks and hallucinations.
AI.method: The researchers propose an enhancement to Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) called Source-Attributed BPE (SA-BPE), which involves modifications like adjusting objectives and employing merge skipping during training.
AI.result: SA-BPE reduces the under-trained tokens caused by data imbalances and repetitive sequences while maintaining compatibility with standard inference processes.
AI.conclusion: The study offers a practical and production-ready solution for more balanced and efficient tokenization in large language models, particularly for code-related applications.

title: A Resource-Efficient Hybrid CNN-LSTM network for image-based bean leaf disease classification
summary: Accurate and resource-efficient automated diagnosis is a cornerstone of modern agricultural expert systems. While Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have established benchmarks in plant pathology, their ability to capture long-range spatial dependencies is often limited by standard pooling layers, and their high memory footprint hinders deployment on portable devices. This paper proposes a lightweight hybrid CNN-LSTM system for bean leaf disease classification. By integrating an LSTM layer to model the spatial-sequential relationships within feature maps, our hybrid architecture achieves a 94.38% accuracy while maintaining an exceptionally small footprint of 1.86 MB; a 70% reduction in size compared to traditional CNN-based systems. Furthermore, we provide a systematic evaluation of image augmentation strategies, demonstrating that tailored transformations are superior to generic combinations for maintaining the integrity of diagnostic patterns. Results on the $\textit{ibean}$ dataset confirm that the proposed system achieves state-of-the-art F1 scores of 99.22% with EfficientNet-B7+LSTM, providing a robust and scalable framework for real-time agricultural decision support in resource-constrained environments. The code and augmented datasets used in this study are publicly available on this $\href{https://github.com/HJin-R/bean_disease}{Github}$ repo.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a lightweight hybrid CNN-LSTM model for bean leaf disease classification, achieving 94.38% accuracy with only 1.86 MB model size and state-of-the-art F1 score of 99.22%, optimized for resource-constrained environments.
AI.motivation: To enable accurate and resource-efficient automated diagnosis in modern agricultural systems, overcoming the challenges of CNNs' limited ability to capture long-range dependencies and their high memory requirements.
AI.method: The proposed method integrates CNNs with an LSTM layer to model spatial-sequential relationships within feature maps. It also evaluates tailored image augmentation strategies for preserving diagnostic features.
AI.result: The hybrid CNN-LSTM system shows 94.38% accuracy, reduces model size by 70% to 1.86 MB, and achieves state-of-the-art F1 score of 99.22% on the ibean dataset.
AI.conclusion: The system offers a robust, scalable, and resource-efficient framework for real-time agricultural decision-making, suitable for portable and resource-constrained devices.

title: Beyond State Consistency: Behavior Consistency in Text-Based World Models
summary: World models have been emerging as critical components for assessing the consequences of actions generated by interactive agents in online planning and offline evaluation. In text-based environments, world models are typically evaluated and trained with single-step metrics such as Exact Match, aiming to improve the similarity between predicted and real-world states, but such metrics have been shown to be insufficient for capturing actual agent behavior. To address this issue, we introduce a new behavior-aligned training paradigm aimed at improving the functional consistency between the world model and the real environment. This paradigm focuses on optimizing a tractable step-level metric named Behavior Consistency Reward (BehR), which measures how much the likelihood of a logged next action changes between the real state and the world-model-predicted state under a frozen Reference Agent. Experiments on WebShop and TextWorld show that BehR-based training improves long-term alignment in several settings, with the clearest gains in WebShop and less movement in near-ceiling regimes, while preserving or improving single-step prediction quality in three of four settings. World models trained with BehR also achieve lower false positives in offline surrogate evaluation and show modest but encouraging gains in inference-time lookahead planning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Behavior Consistency Reward (BehR), a new training paradigm for improving long-term consistency between world models and real environments in text-based interactive settings. Experiments show that BehR enhances agent behavior alignment while preserving single-step prediction quality.
AI.motivation: To resolve the inadequacy of single-step metrics like Exact Match for evaluating and training world models in text-based environments to improve agent behavior alignment.
AI.method: The authors propose optimizing Behavior Consistency Reward (BehR)—a tractable step-level metric that quantifies the change in logged next-action likelihood between the real state and the world-model-predicted state under a frozen Reference Agent.
AI.result: Using BehR for training results in improved long-term alignment in world models, especially in WebShop tasks, with better offline surrogate evaluation and minor gains in inference-time lookahead planning.
AI.conclusion: The novel BehR-based paradigm enhances functional consistency between predicted and real-world states, advancing world model training while retaining single-step prediction accuracy.

title: From Weights to Activations: Is Steering the Next Frontier of Adaptation?
summary:
Post-training adaptation of language models is commonly achieved through parameter updates or input-based methods such as fine-tuning, parameter-efficient adaptation, and prompting. In parallel, a growing body of work modifies internal activations at inference time to influence model behavior, an approach known as steering. Despite increasing use, steering is rarely analyzed within the same conceptual framework as established adaptation methods.
  In this work, we argue that steering should be regarded as a form of model adaptation. We introduce a set of functional criteria for adaptation methods and use them to compare steering approaches with classical alternatives. This analysis positions steering as a distinct adaptation paradigm based on targeted interventions in activation space, enabling local and reversible behavioral change without parameter updates. The resulting framing clarifies how steering relates to existing methods, motivating a unified taxonomy for model adaptation.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that steering, which modifies internal activations at inference time, should be considered as a form of model adaptation akin to fine-tuning and prompting, and proposes a unified framework for analyzing adaptation methods.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the lack of a unified conceptual framework that includes steering together with conventional adaptation methods for language models.
AI.method: The authors introduce functional criteria for adaptation methods and compare steering with classical methods, highlighting its unique paradigm of activation-space intervention.
AI.result: Steering is positioned as enabling localized and reversible behavioral changes without necessitating parameter updates, distinguishing it from other forms of adaptation.
AI.conclusion: The work proposes a unified taxonomy for model adaptation that encompasses steering, offering clarity on its relationship with traditional methods.

title: DiffMagicFace: Identity Consistent Facial Editing of Real Videos
summary: Text-conditioned image editing has greatly benefitted from the advancements in Image Diffusion Models. However, extending these techniques to facial video editing introduces challenges in preserving facial identity throughout the source video and ensuring consistency of the edited subject across frames. In this paper, we introduce DiffMagicFace, a unique video editing framework that integrates two fine-tuned models for text and image control. These models operate concurrently during inference to produce video frames that maintain identity features while seamlessly aligning with the editing semantics. To ensure the consistency of the edited videos, we develop a dataset comprising images showcasing various facial perspectives for each edited subject. The creation of a data set is achieved through rendering techniques and the subsequent application of optimization algorithms. Remarkably, our approach does not depend on video datasets but still delivers high-quality results in both consistency and content. The excellent effect holds even for complex tasks like talking head videos and distinguishing closely related categories. The videos edited using our framework exhibit parity with videos that are made using traditional rendering software. Through comparative analysis with current state-of-the-art methods, our framework demonstrates superior performance in both visual appeal and quantitative metrics.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DiffMagicFace, a text-guided framework for video editing that ensures identity preservation and frame consistency, outperforming existing methods without relying on video datasets.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the challenges in text-conditioned facial video editing, particularly preserving facial identity and ensuring consistency across frames.
AI.method: The framework integrates two finely tuned models operating concurrently during inference for text and image control. Additionally, a novel dataset showcasing facial perspectives is created using rendering and optimization techniques.
AI.result: The framework delivers consistent and high-quality edited videos without relying on video datasets, excelling in difficult tasks like talking head videos and closely related category distinctions.
AI.conclusion: DiffMagicFace surpasses state-of-the-art methods by delivering visually appealing and quantitatively accurate edited videos, achieving results comparable to traditional rendering methods.

title: SparseBalance: Load-Balanced Long Context Training with Dynamic Sparse Attention
summary: While sparse attention mitigates the computational bottleneck of long-context LLM training, its distributed training process exhibits extreme heterogeneity in both \textit{1)} sequence length and \textit{2)} sparsity sensitivity, leading to a severe imbalance problem and sub-optimal model accuracy. Existing algorithms and training frameworks typically focus on single issue, failing to systematically co-optimize these two problems. Therefore, we propose SparseBalance, a novel algorithm-system co-design framework, which exploits the sparsity and sequence heterogeneity to optimize model accuracy and system efficiency jointly. First, we propose workload-aware dynamic sparsity tuning, which employs a bidirectional sparsity adjustment to eliminate stragglers and exploit inherent bubbles for free accuracy. Second, we propose a sparsity-aware batching strategy to achieve coarse-grained balance, which complements dynamic sparsity tuning. Experimental results demonstrate that SparseBalance achieves up to a 1.33$\times$ end-to-end speedup while still improving the long-context capability by 0.46\% on the LongBench benchmark.

AI.tldr: SparseBalance framework resolves the imbalance challenges in sparse attention during distributed training by addressing issues in sequence length heterogeneity and sparsity sensitivity.
AI.motivation: Addressing extreme heterogeneity in sequence lengths and sparsity sensitivity during sparse attention training for better accuracy and system efficiency.
AI.method: SparseBalance integrates workload-aware dynamic sparsity tuning and sparsity-aware batching strategy to resolve heterogeneity problems jointly.
AI.result: SparseBalance yields a 1.33× speedup and a 0.46% improvement in long-context capability on the LongBench benchmark.
AI.conclusion: SparseBalance successfully enhances both training efficiency and model accuracy for long-context sparse attention tasks.

title: Interpretable Stylistic Variation in Human and LLM Writing Across Genres, Models, and Decoding Strategies
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are now capable of generating highly fluent, human-like text. They enable many applications, but also raise concerns such as large scale spam, phishing, or academic misuse. While much work has focused on detecting LLM-generated text, only limited work has gone into understanding the stylistic differences between human-written and machine-generated text. In this work, we perform a large scale analysis of stylistic variation across human-written text and outputs from 11 LLMs spanning 8 different genres and 4 decoding strategies using Douglas Biber's set of lexicogrammatical and functional features. Our findings reveal insights that can guide intentional LLM usage. First, key linguistic differentiators of LLM-generated text seem robust to generation conditions (e.g., prompt settings to nudge them to generate human-like text, or availability of human-written text to continue the style); second, genre exerts a stronger influence on stylistic features than the source itself; third, chat variants of the models generally appear to be clustered together in stylistic space, and finally, model has a larger effect on the style than decoding strategy, with some exceptions. These results highlight the relative importance of model and genre over prompting and decoding strategies in shaping the stylistic behavior of machine-generated text.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes stylistic differences between human-written and LLM-generated text using linguistic features, revealing insights about the influence of genre and model over prompting and decoding strategies.
AI.motivation: The authors are driven by the need to understand stylistic differences between human-written and LLM-generated text, which can help mitigate malicious uses and improve intentional LLM deployment.
AI.method: The authors analyzed text generated by 11 LLMs across 8 genres and 4 decoding strategies using Douglas Biber's lexicogrammatical and functional features to identify stylistic variations.
AI.result: Key findings include (1) linguistic differences between human- and machine-generated text are robust to generation settings, (2) genre significantly impacts stylistic features, (3) chat models cluster stylistically, and (4) the model choice has a larger effect on style than decoding strategies.
AI.conclusion: Genre and model type are critical factors shaping the style of machine-generated text, while prompting and decoding strategies have relatively minor effects.

title: Any3DAvatar: Fast and High-Quality Full-Head 3D Avatar Reconstruction from Single Portrait Image
summary: Reconstructing a complete 3D head from a single portrait remains challenging because existing methods still face a sharp quality-speed trade-off: high-fidelity pipelines often rely on multi-stage processing and per-subject optimization, while fast feed-forward models struggle with complete geometry and fine appearance details. To bridge this gap, we propose Any3DAvatar, a fast and high-quality method for single-image 3D Gaussian head avatar generation, whose fastest setting reconstructs a full head in under one second while preserving high-fidelity geometry and texture. First, we build AnyHead, a unified data suite that combines identity diversity, dense multi-view supervision, and realistic accessories, filling the main gaps of existing head data in coverage, full-head geometry, and complex appearance. Second, rather than sampling unstructured noise, we initialize from a Plücker-aware structured 3D Gaussian scaffold and perform one-step conditional denoising, formulating full-head reconstruction into a single forward pass while retaining high fidelity. Third, we introduce auxiliary view-conditioned appearance supervision on the same latent tokens alongside 3D Gaussian reconstruction, improving novel-view texture details at zero extra inference cost. Experiments show that Any3DAvatar outperforms prior single-image full-head reconstruction methods in rendering fidelity while remaining substantially faster.

AI.tldr: Proposes Any3DAvatar, a fast method for generating 3D head avatars from a single image, achieving speed and quality simultaneously.
AI.motivation: The need to overcome the quality-speed trade-off in single-image 3D head reconstruction. Current methods are either slow and high-fidelity or fast with poor geometry and texture quality.
AI.method: Developed AnyHead, a diverse head data suite, and applied structured 3D Gaussian scaffolds with one-step conditional denoising for reconstruction. Introduced auxiliary view-conditioned texture supervision.
AI.result: Achieved high fidelity and improved texture details under one second on average, outperforming previous methods in speed and accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Any3DAvatar successfully bridges the gap between quality and speed in 3D head reconstruction, achieving high fidelity with efficiency.

title: Simulation-Based Optimisation of Batting Order and Bowling Plans in T20 Cricket
comment: Submitted to the Journal of Quantitative Analysis in Sports (JQAS), April 2026. 23 pages, 8 figures
summary: This paper develops a unified Markov Decision Process (MDP) framework for optimising two recurring in-match decisions in T20 cricket namely batting order selection and bowling plan assignment, directly in terms of win and defend probability rather than expected runs. A three-phase player profile engine (Powerplay, Middle, Death) with James-Stein shrinkage is estimated from 1,161 IPL ball-by-ball records (2008-2025). Win/defend probabilities are evaluated by vectorised Monte Carlo simulation over N = 50,000 innings trajectories. Batting orders are searched by exhaustive enumeration. Bowling plans are computed by simulated annealing over the remaining quota with the constraint that the same bowler cannot bowl consecutive overs. Applied to two 2026 IPL matches, the optimal batting order improves Mumbai Indians' win probability by 4.1 percentage points (52.4% to 56.5%), and the optimal Gujarat Titans bowling plan improves defend probability by 5.2 percentage points (39.1% to 44.3%). In both cases the observed sub-optimality is consistent with phase-agnostic deployment in decisions that appear reasonable by aggregate metrics but are exposed as costly when phase-specific profiles are applied.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a Markov Decision Process (MDP) model to optimize batting orders and bowling plans for T20 cricket using win probabilities rather than runs. It evaluates player profiles and trajectories for strategy improvement.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the gap in optimizing T20 cricket decisions using win probabilities over expected runs, aiming to enhance in-match strategies for batting order and bowling plans.
AI.method: It uses a player profile engine (split by phases) from IPL data, vectorised Monte Carlo simulations, exhaustive enumeration, and simulated annealing with constraints like bowling continuity.
AI.result: The optimal strategies improve win probability for Mumbai Indians by 4.1 percentage points, and defend probability for Gujarat Titans by 5.2 percentage points in IPL 2026 matches.
AI.conclusion: The findings show phase-agnostic strategies as suboptimal when assessed with phase-specific profiles, demonstrating the benefits of this MDP framework for strategic optimizations in T20 cricket.

title: Correct Prediction, Wrong Steps? Consensus Reasoning Knowledge Graph for Robust Chain-of-Thought Synthesis
summary: LLM reasoning traces suffer from complex flaws -- *Step Internal Flaws* (logical errors, hallucinations, etc.) and *Step-wise Flaws* (overthinking, underthinking), which vary by sample. A natural approach would be to provide ground-truth labels to guide LLMs' reasoning. Contrary to intuition, we show that this yields no improvement in reasoning ability. We then propose CRAFT, a unified framework that mitigates both types of Step flaws, which builds a Reasoning Knowledge Graph (RKG) based on the consensus parts of multiple candidate traces, and synthesizes a high-quality trace through topological generation. Our approach improves label-prediction accuracy by 10+% on average, and consistently outperforms all baselines across both logical and mathematical reasoning benchmarks. Further, detailed benchmark evaluation proves that our method also improves the quality of LLMs' reasoning traces in multiple dimensions.

AI.tldr: LLM reasoning traces are plagued by logical and step-wise flaws, and the proposed CRAFT framework addresses these by using a Reasoning Knowledge Graph for more consistent reasoning.
AI.motivation: Despite providing ground-truth labels to LLMs, their reasoning ability doesn't improve, revealing the necessity of a better framework to resolve internal logical and step-wise flaws.
AI.method: The CRAFT framework constructs a Reasoning Knowledge Graph from the consensus of multiple reasoning traces and synthesizes an improved reasoning trace through topological methods.
AI.result: CRAFT improves label-prediction accuracy by over 10%, and outperforms baselines in logical and mathematical reasoning benchmarks, enhancing reasoning trace quality.
AI.conclusion: The proposed CRAFT framework effectively mitigates reasoning flaws in LLMs, enhancing their reasoning capabilities and trace quality across multiple benchmarks.

title: PostureObjectstitch: Anomaly Image Generation Considering Assembly Relationships in Industrial Scenarios
summary: Image generation technology can synthesize condition-specific images to supplement real-world industrial anomaly data and enhance anomaly detection model performance. Existing generation techniques rarely account for the pose and orientation of industrial components in assembly, making the generated images difficult to utilize for downstream application. To solve this, we propose a novel image synthesis approach, called PostureObjectStitch, that achieves accurate generation to meet the requirement of industrial assembly. A condition decoupling approach is introduced to separate input multi-view images into high-frequency, texture, and RGB features. The feature temporal modulation mechanism adapts these features across diffusion model time-steps, enabling progressive generation from coarse to fine details while maintaining consistency. To ensure semantic accuracy, we introduce a conditional loss that enhances critical industrial elements and a geometric prior that guides component positioning for correct assembly relationships. Comprehensive experimental results on the MureCom dataset, our newly contributed DreamAssembly dataset, and the downstream application validate the outstanding performance of our method.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces PostureObjectStitch, a new method for generating realistic industrial images while preserving pose and orientation, utilizing feature decoupling and a diffusion model.
AI.motivation: Current image generation methods struggle to consider pose and orientation of industrial components, reducing their utility for tasks like anomaly detection in industrial settings.
AI.method: The proposed PostureObjectStitch employs condition decoupling to segregate high-frequency, texture, and RGB features and uses feature temporal modulation within a diffusion model for progressive image generation.
AI.result: Experiments on the MureCom and DreamAssembly datasets demonstrate superior performance in generating realistic, pose-accurate images and improving downstream applications like anomaly detection.
AI.conclusion: PostureObjectStitch effectively addresses the limitation of pose accuracy in image generation, enhancing its applicability for industrial anomaly detection and related tasks.

title: Hardware-Efficient Neuro-Symbolic Networks with the Exp-Minus-Log Operator
summary: Deep neural networks (DNNs) deliver state-of-the-art accuracy on regression and classification tasks, yet two structural deficits persistently obstruct their deployment in safety-critical, resource-constrained settings: (i) opacity of the learned function, which precludes formal verification, and (ii) reliance on heterogeneous, library-bound activation functions that inflate latency and silicon area on edge hardware. The recently introduced Exp-Minus-Log (EML) Sheffer operator, eml(x, y) = exp(x) - ln(y), was shown by Odrzywolek (2026) to be sufficient - together with the constant 1 - to express every standard elementary function as a binary tree of identical nodes. We propose to embed EML primitives inside conventional DNN architectures, yielding a hybrid DNN-EML model in which the trunk learns distributed representations and the head is a depth-bounded, weight-sparse EML tree whose snapped weights collapse to closed-form symbolic sub-expressions. We derive the forward equations, prove computational-cost bounds, analyse inference and training acceleration relative to multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) and physics-informed neural networks (PINNs), and quantify the trade-offs for FPGA/analog deployment. We argue that the DNN-EML pairing closes a literature gap: prior neuro-symbolic and equation-learner approaches (EQL, KAN, AI-Feynman) work with heterogeneous primitive sets and do not exploit a single hardware-realisable Sheffer element. A balanced assessment shows that EML is unlikely to accelerate training, and on commodity CPU/GPU it is also unlikely to accelerate inference; however, on a custom EML cell (FPGA logic block or analog circuit) the asymptotic latency advantage can reach an order of magnitude with simultaneous gain in interpretability and formal-verification tractability.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a hybrid neural network model incorporating the Exp-Minus-Log (EML) operator to address opacity and hardware inefficiency in deep neural networks.
AI.motivation: DNNs face challenges in safety-critical and resource-constrained environments due to their opaque learned functions and reliance on hardware-inefficient activation functions.
AI.method: The authors propose embedding EML primitives in DNN architectures to create a weight-sparse EML tree for the head, leveraging its symbolic representation capabilities.
AI.result: The EML-enabled model improves interpretability and supports formal verification while potentially reducing latency on specialized FPGA/analog hardware.
AI.conclusion: While training and inference may not accelerate on traditional CPU/GPU setups, deploying EML models on custom hardware offers significant latency benefits without compromising accuracy.

title: Rhetorical Questions in LLM Representations: A Linear Probing Study
summary: Rhetorical questions are asked not to seek information but to persuade or signal stance. How large language models internally represent them remains unclear. We analyze rhetorical questions in LLM representations using linear probes on two social-media datasets with different discourse contexts, and find that rhetorical signals emerge early and are most stably captured by last-token representations. Rhetorical questions are linearly separable from information-seeking questions within datasets, and remain detectable under cross-dataset transfer, reaching AUROC around 0.7-0.8. However, we demonstrate that transferability does not simply imply a shared representation. Probes trained on different datasets produce different rankings when applied to the same target corpus, with overlap among the top-ranked instances often below 0.2. Qualitative analysis shows that these divergences correspond to distinct rhetorical phenomena: some probes capture discourse-level rhetorical stance embedded in extended argumentation, while others emphasize localized, syntax-driven interrogative acts. Together, these findings suggest that rhetorical questions in LLM representations are encoded by multiple linear directions emphasizing different cues, rather than a single shared direction.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how large language models (LLMs) internally represent rhetorical questions using linear probes and finds that these signals are detectable and encoded in diverse ways.
AI.motivation: To explore how rhetorical questions, which serve persuasive or stance-signaling purposes, are internally represented in large language models, given their crucial role in discourse and communication.
AI.method: The authors used linear probes to analyze rhetorical questions in LLMs across two distinct social-media datasets. They evaluated the separability of rhetorical from information-seeking questions and examined cross-dataset transfer performance.
AI.result: The study finds that rhetorical signals are stably detected early in processing, with last-token representations performing best for classification (AUROC 0.7-0.8). Cross-dataset transfer works but shows significant divergences in instance rankings and rhetorical phenomena captured across datasets.
AI.conclusion: Rhetorical questions are not encoded as a singular shared representation in LLMs but rather across multiple linear directions, emphasizing different discourse or syntax-based cues.

title: From Feelings to Metrics: Understanding and Formalizing How Users Vibe-Test LLMs
comment: TLDR: Under review. 42 pages, 18 figures. Code and data at https://itay1itzhak.github.io/vibe-testing-llms Subjects: Computation and Language (cs.CL); Machine Learning (cs.LG)
summary: Evaluating LLMs is challenging, as benchmark scores often fail to capture models' real-world usefulness. Instead, users often rely on ``vibe-testing'': informal experience-based evaluation, such as comparing models on coding tasks related to their own workflow. While prevalent, vibe-testing is often too ad hoc and unstructured to analyze or reproduce at scale. In this work, we study how vibe-testing works in practice and then formalize it to support systematic analysis. We first analyze two empirical resources: (1) a survey of user evaluation practices, and (2) a collection of in-the-wild model comparison reports from blogs and social media. Based on these resources, we formalize vibe-testing as a two-part process: users personalize both what they test and how they judge responses. We then introduce a proof-of-concept evaluation pipeline that follows this formulation by generating personalized prompts and comparing model outputs using user-aware subjective criteria. In experiments on coding benchmarks, we find that combining personalized prompts and user-aware evaluation can change which model is preferred, reflecting the role of vibe-testing in practice. These findings suggest that formalized vibe-testing can serve as a useful approach for bridging benchmark scores and real-world experience.

AI.tldr: Benchmark scores often miss the real-world utility of LLMs, leading users to rely on informal evaluations like "vibe-testing." This study formalizes these ad hoc practices for systematic analysis.
AI.motivation: Current evaluations of LLMs fail to capture their real-world utility, pushing users toward informal and subjective testing methods that lack structure and scalability.
AI.method: The paper analyzes user evaluation practices and online reports, then formalizes "vibe-testing" as a personalized and subjective process. A proof-of-concept evaluation pipeline is introduced, featuring personalized prompts and user-aware criteria.
AI.result: Experiments show that personalized testing and evaluation criteria can influence model preferences, highlighting the gap between benchmarks and practical use.
AI.conclusion: Formalizing vibe-testing provides a structured way to evaluate LLMs' real-world usability, bridging the gap between benchmarks and user experience.

title: Rethinking Image-to-3D Generation with Sparse Queries: Efficiency, Capacity, and Input-View Bias
summary: We present SparseGen, a novel framework for efficient image-to-3D generation, which exhibits low input-view bias while being significantly faster. Unlike traditional approaches that rely on dense volumetric grids, triplanes, or pixel-aligned primitives, we model scenes with a compact sparse set of learned 3D anchor queries and a learned expansion operator that decodes each transformed query into a small local set of 3D Gaussian primitives. Trained under a rectified-flow reconstruction objective without 3D supervision, our model learns to allocate representation capacity where geometry and appearance matter, achieving significant reductions in memory and inference time while preserving multi-view fidelity. We introduce quantitative measures of input-view bias and utilization to show that sparse queries reduce overfitting to conditioning views while being representationally efficient. Our results argue that sparse set-latent expansion is a principled, practical alternative for efficient 3D generative modeling.

AI.tldr: SparseGen uses sparse 3D anchor queries with Gaussian primitives to efficiently create 3D models, reducing memory use and input-view bias while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: Address input-view bias and inefficiency in traditional image-to-3D generation methods.
AI.method: Uses sparse learned 3D anchor queries and a learned expansion operator, optimized via a rectified-flow reconstruction method without 3D supervision.
AI.result: Improved memory usage, faster inference, reduced input-view bias, and efficient 3D generative modeling.
AI.conclusion: Sparse set-latent expansion is a viable, efficient alternative for 3D generative tasks.

title: Evaluating Supervised Machine Learning Models: Principles, Pitfalls, and Metric Selection
summary: The evaluation of supervised machine learning models is a critical stage in the development of reliable predictive systems. Despite the widespread availability of machine learning libraries and automated workflows, model assessment is often reduced to the reporting of a small set of aggregate metrics, which can lead to misleading conclusions about real-world performance. This paper examines the principles, challenges, and practical considerations involved in evaluating supervised learning algorithms across classification and regression tasks. In particular, it discusses how evaluation outcomes are influenced by dataset characteristics, validation design, class imbalance, asymmetric error costs, and the choice of performance metrics. Through a series of controlled experimental scenarios using diverse benchmark datasets, the study highlights common pitfalls such as the accuracy paradox, data leakage, inappropriate metric selection, and overreliance on scalar summary measures. The paper also compares alternative validation strategies and emphasizes the importance of aligning model evaluation with the intended operational objective of the task. By presenting evaluation as a decision-oriented and context-dependent process, this work provides a structured foundation for selecting metrics and validation protocols that support statistically sound, robust, and trustworthy supervised machine learning systems.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the pitfalls and best practices in evaluating supervised machine learning models by discussing principles, challenges, and contextual considerations.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to improve the reliability of model evaluations, addressing the misleading results caused by aggregate metrics and common evaluation flaws.
AI.method: Controlled experiments were performed on diverse benchmark datasets to explore how dataset characteristics, validation designs, and metric choices impact evaluation outcomes.
AI.result: The study highlights evaluation challenges such as accuracy paradox, data leakage, improper metric selection, and overreliance on scalar metrics while recommending better practices.
AI.conclusion: Model evaluation should be a context-aware, decision-oriented process, integrating suitable validation strategies and metrics for robust machine learning systems.

title: Blind Bitstream-corrupted Video Recovery via Metadata-guided Diffusion Model
summary: Bitstream-corrupted video recovery aims to restore realistic content degraded during video storage or transmission. Existing methods typically assume that predefined masks of corrupted regions are available, but manually annotating these masks is labor-intensive and impractical in real-world scenarios. To address this limitation, we introduce a new blind video recovery setting that removes the reliance on predefined masks. This setting presents two major challenges: accurately identifying corrupted regions and recovering content from extensive and irregular degradations. We propose a Metadata-Guided Diffusion Model (M-GDM) to tackle these challenges. Specifically, intrinsic video metadata are leveraged as corruption indicators through a dual-stream metadata encoder that separately embeds motion vectors and frame types before fusing them into a unified representation. This representation interacts with corrupted latent features via cross-attention at each diffusion step. To preserve intact regions, we design a prior-driven mask predictor that generates pseudo masks using both metadata and diffusion priors, enabling the separation and recombination of intact and recovered regions through hard masking. To mitigate boundary artifacts caused by imperfect masks, a post-refinement module enhances consistency between intact and recovered regions. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our method and its superiority in blind video recovery. Code is available at: https://github.com/Shuyun-Wang/M-GDM.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a Metadata-Guided Diffusion Model (M-GDM) for blind video recovery, which does not require predefined masks to identify corrupted regions. It effectively detects and repairs corrupted video areas using video metadata, pseudo mask predictors, and refinement modules.
AI.motivation: Existing video recovery methods depend on predefined masks for corrupted regions, which are impractical to generate manually in real-world applications. Hence, the authors aim to create a more feasible and automated solution for video recovery.
AI.method: The authors propose the Metadata-Guided Diffusion Model (M-GDM), which utilizes video metadata (motion vectors and frame types) via a dual-stream metadata encoder to identify corruption. They introduce a prior-driven mask predictor to generate pseudo masks and a post-refinement module to enhance recovery consistency.
AI.result: Experiments show that the M-GDM outperforms existing methods in blind video recovery tasks, demonstrating superior accuracy and quality in recovering corrupted videos.
AI.conclusion: The proposed M-GDM provides a feasible, automated, and effective solution for blind video recovery by leveraging metadata indicators, pseudo masks, and refinement techniques. It eliminates the need for predefined masks and achieves state-of-the-art results.

title: MolCryst-MLIPs: A Machine-Learned Interatomic Potentials Database for Molecular Crystals
summary: We present an open Molecular Crystal (MC) database of Machine-Learned Interatomic Potentials (MLIP) called MolCryst-MLIPs. The first release comprises fine-tuned MACE models for nine molecular crystal systems -- Benzamide, Benzoic acid, Coumarin, Durene, Isonicotinamide, Niacinamide, Nicotinamide, Pyrazinamide, and Resorcinol -- developed using the Automated Machine Learning Pipeline (AMLP), which streamlines the entire MLIP development workflow, from reference data generation to model training and validation, into a reproducible and user-friendly pipeline. Models are fine-tuned from the MACE-MH-1 foundation model (omol head), yielding a mean energy MAE of 0.141 kJ/mol/atom and a mean force MAE of 0.648 kJ/mol/Angstrom across all systems. Dynamical stability and structural integrity, as assessed through energy conservation, P2 orientational order parameters, and radial distribution functions, are evaluated using molecular dynamics simulations. The released models and datasets constitute a growing open database of validated MLIPs, ready for production MD simulations of molecular crystal polymorphism under different thermodynamic conditions.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces MolCryst-MLIPs, an open database of machine-learned interatomic potentials specifically tailored for molecular crystals. The initial release focuses on nine systems and achieves high accuracy in energy and force predictions.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to provide a reproducible, user-friendly, and validated database of MLIP models for molecular crystals, enabling accurate molecular dynamics simulations across varying conditions.
AI.method: The method involves using the Automated Machine Learning Pipeline (AMLP) to generate fine-tuned MACE models from the MACE-MH-1 foundation model. Validation is performed through metrics like energy MAE, force MAE, and molecular dynamics simulations.
AI.result: The fine-tuned models produced a mean energy MAE of 0.141 kJ/mol/atom and a mean force MAE of 0.648 kJ/mol/Angstrom across nine molecular crystal systems. Structural and dynamical stabilities were confirmed via simulations.
AI.conclusion: The MolCryst-MLIPs database provides highly accurate, validated MLIP models for studying molecular crystal polymorphism and is ready for production use in molecular dynamic simulations.

title: Finetuning-Free Diffusion Model with Adaptive Constraint Guidance for Inorganic Crystal Structure Generation
comment: Full article including supplementary information, 55 pages, 9 figures
summary:
The discovery of inorganic crystal structures with targeted properties is a significant challenge in materials science. Generative models, especially state-of-the-art diffusion models, offer the promise of modeling complex data distributions and proposing novel, realistic samples. However, current generative AI models still struggle to produce diverse, original, and reliable structures of experimentally achievable materials suitable for high-stakes applications.
  In this work, we propose a generative machine learning framework based on diffusion models with adaptive constraint guidance, which enables the incorporation of user-defined physical and chemical constraints during the generation process. This approach is designed to be practical and interpretable for human experts, allowing transparent decision-making and expert-driven exploration. To ensure the robustness and validity of the generated candidates, we introduce a multi-step validation pipeline that combines graph neural network estimators trained to achieve DFT-level accuracy and convex hull analysis for assessing thermodynamic stability. Our approach has been tested and validated on several classical examples of inorganic families of compounds, as case studies. As a consequence, these preliminary results demonstrate our framework's ability to generate thermodynamically plausible crystal structures that satisfy targeted geometric constraints across diverse inorganic chemical systems.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a diffusion-based generative AI framework to model inorganic crystal structures while incorporating physical and chemical constraints for reliable and interpretable material design.
AI.motivation: The challenge in materials science lies in discovering inorganic crystal structures with specific properties, as generative models often fail to create diverse and experimentally feasible candidates for high-stakes applications.
AI.method: The authors propose a diffusion model with adaptive constraint guidance, integrating user-defined constraints during generation, and validate the samples using graph neural network estimators and thermodynamic stability analysis.
AI.result: The framework was validated on several inorganic compound families, successfully generating thermodynamically plausible and geometrically constrained crystal structures.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework offers an interpretable, constraint-guided generative approach for material design, capable of producing realistic, thermodynamically viable inorganic structures.

title: PartNerFace: Part-based Neural Radiance Fields for Animatable Facial Avatar Reconstruction
summary: We present PartNerFace, a part-based neural radiance fields approach, for reconstructing animatable facial avatar from monocular RGB videos. Existing solutions either simply condition the implicit network with the morphable model parameters or learn an imaginary canonical radiance field, making them fail to generalize to unseen facial expressions and capture fine-scale motion details. To address these challenges, we first apply inverse skinning based on a parametric head model to map an observed point to the canonical space, and then model fine-scale motions with a part-based deformation field. Our key insight is that the deformation of different facial parts should be modeled differently. Specifically, our part-based deformation field consists of multiple local MLPs to adaptively partition the canonical space into different parts, where the deformation of a 3D point is computed by aggregating the prediction of all local MLPs by a soft-weighting mechanism. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method generalizes well to unseen expressions and is capable of modeling fine-scale facial motions, outperforming state-of-the-art methods both quantitatively and qualitatively.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PartNerFace, a new part-based neural radiance field approach for animatable facial avatar reconstruction from mono RGB videos, improving generalization to unseen expressions and capturing fine motion details.
AI.motivation: Existing methods struggle with generalizing to unseen expressions and precisely modeling fine-scale facial details. The paper aims to address these limitations by introducing a part-based modeling framework.
AI.method: The approach employs inverse skinning using a parametric head model to map observed points to canonical space and models fine-scale motions using part-based deformation fields. Each part's deformation is handled by multiple local MLPs with a soft-weighting aggregation mechanism.
AI.result: Experiments prove that the method better adapts to unseen expressions and finely captures facial motion details compared to state-of-the-art techniques, achieving superior qualitative and quantitative results.
AI.conclusion: PartNerFace effectively addresses the drawbacks of existing solutions, offering improved performance in animatable facial avatar reconstruction from monocular RGB videos.

title: DiPO: Disentangled Perplexity Policy Optimization for Fine-grained Exploration-Exploitation Trade-Off
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has catalyzed significant advances in the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). However, effectively managing the exploration and exploitation trade-off remains a critical challenge. In this paper, we fully analyze the exploration and exploitation dilemma of extremely hard and easy samples during the training and propose a new fine-grained trade-off mechanism. Concretely, we introduce a perplexity space disentangling strategy that divides the sample space into distinct exploration (high perplexity) and exploitation (low perplexity) subspaces, thereby mining fine-grained samples requiring exploration-exploitation trade-off. Subsequently, we propose a bidirectional reward allocation mechanism with a minimum impact on verification rewards to implement perplexity-guided exploration and exploitation, enabling more stable policy optimization. Finally, we have evaluated our method on two mainstream tasks: mathematical reasoning and function calling, and experimental results demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method, confirming its effectiveness in enhancing LLM performance by fine-grained exploration-exploitation trade-off.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a method to tackle the exploration-exploitation trade-off in Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) by introducing a perplexity-based disentangling strategy and finer reward allocation mechanism, which enhances LLM performance.
AI.motivation: The exploration-exploitation trade-off in RLVR poses challenges when dealing with extremely hard or easy samples, limiting the reasoning capabilities of LLMs. Addressing this problem ensures more stable and effective policy optimization.
AI.method: The authors develop a perplexity space disentangling strategy to divide samples into high (exploration) and low (exploitation) perplexity subspaces. They further employ a bidirectional reward allocation mechanism to optimize exploration and exploitation in a fine-grained manner, guided by perplexity.
AI.result: The method was tested on mathematical reasoning and function calling tasks, and experimental results highlight improved policy stability and enhanced reasoning capabilities in LLMs.
AI.conclusion: The proposed perplexity-guided exploration-exploitation mechanism effectively improves LLM performance on complex tasks, demonstrating its utility in handling RLVR challenges.

title: From Seeing it to Experiencing it: Interactive Evaluation of Intersectional Voice Bias in Human-AI Speech Interaction
comment: 6 pages, 3 figures, 1 table, Accepted to CHI Extended Abstracts Poster session 2026
summary: SpeechLLMs process spoken language directly from audio, but accent and vocal identity cues can lead to biased behaviour. Current bias evaluations often miss how such bias manifests in end-to-end speech interactions and how users experience it. We distinguish quality-of-service disparities (e.g., off-topic or low-effort responses) from content-level bias in coherent outputs, and examine intersectional effects of accent and perceived gender. In this work, we explore a two-part evaluation approach: (1) a controlled test cohort spanning six accents and two gender presentations, analysed with judge-free prompt-response metrics, and (2) an interactive study design using voice conversion to let users experience identical content through different vocal identities. Across two studies (Interactive, N=24; Observational, N=19), we find that voice conversion increases trust and acceptability for benign responses and encourages perspective-taking, while automated analysis in search of quality-of-service disparities, reveals {accent x gender} disparities in alignment and verbosity across SpeechLLMs. These results highlight voice conversion for probing and experiencing intersectional voice bias while our evaluation suite provides richer bias evaluations for spoken conversational AI.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses bias in SpeechLLMs caused by accent and perceived gender, proposing a two-part evaluation method to better understand and address intersectional voice bias.
AI.motivation: To improve understanding of how bias related to vocal identity, like accent and perceived gender, impacts interactions within SpeechLLMs and user experiences.
AI.method: A two-part evaluation is used: (1) controlled tests across accents and gender presentations with quantitative metrics; (2) interactive voice conversion experiments letting users experience identical content via different vocal identities.
AI.result: Voice conversion boosts trust and perspective-taking in interactions, while automated analysis reveals disparities in alignment and verbosity across accent-gender intersections.
AI.conclusion: Voice conversion and refined evaluation methods enrich bias assessments for SpeechLLMs and support addressing intersectional bias in conversational AI systems.

title: ASTRA: Enhancing Multi-Subject Generation with Retrieval-Augmented Pose Guidance and Disentangled Position Embedding
summary: Subject-driven image generation has shown great success in creating personalized content, but its capabilities are largely confined to single subjects in common poses. Current approaches face a fundamental conflict when handling multiple subjects with complex, distinct actions: preserving individual identities while enforcing precise pose structures. This challenge often leads to identity fusion and pose distortion, as appearance and structure signals become entangled within the model's architecture. To resolve this conflict, we introduce ASTRA(Adaptive Synthesis through Targeted Retrieval Augmentation), a novel framework that architecturally disentangles subject appearance from pose structure within a unified Diffusion Transformer. ASTRA achieves this through a dual-pronged strategy. It first employs a Retrieval-Augmented Pose (RAG-Pose) pipeline to provide a clean, explicit structural prior from a curated database. Then, its core generative model learns to process these dual visual conditions using our Enhanced Universal Rotary Position Embedding (EURoPE), an asymmetric encoding mechanism that decouples identity tokens from spatial locations while binding pose tokens to the canvas. Concurrently, a Disentangled Semantic Modulation (DSM) adapter offloads the identity preservation task into the text conditioning stream. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our integrated approach achieves superior disentanglement. On our designed COCO-based complex pose benchmark, ASTRA achieves a new state-of-the-art in pose adherence, while maintaining high identity fidelity and text alignment in DreamBench.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ASTRA, a framework to improve multi-subject, complex-pose image generation by disentangling subject appearance from pose structure.
AI.motivation: To overcome the challenges faced by current methods in generating images with multiple distinct subjects and complex poses without identity fusion or pose distortion.
AI.method: ASTRA employs a Retrieval-Augmented Pose (RAG-Pose) pipeline for explicit structural priors and uses Enhanced Universal Rotary Position Embedding (EURoPE) to encode identity and pose separately. A Disentangled Semantic Modulation (DSM) adapter manages identity preservation in the text stream.
AI.result: Experiments show that ASTRA achieves state-of-the-art performance in both pose adherence and identity preservation, tested on a COCO-based benchmark and DreamBench.
AI.conclusion: The proposed ASTRA framework effectively disentangles identity and pose information, enabling personalized content generation with multiple subjects in complex actions.

title: ASTER: Latent Pseudo-Anomaly Generation for Unsupervised Time-Series Anomaly Detection
summary: Time-series anomaly detection (TSAD) is critical in domains such as industrial monitoring, healthcare, and cybersecurity, but it remains challenging due to rare and heterogeneous anomalies and the scarcity of labelled data. This scarcity makes unsupervised approaches predominant, yet existing methods often rely on reconstruction or forecasting, which struggle with complex data, or on embedding-based approaches that require domain-specific anomaly synthesis and fixed distance metrics. We propose ASTER, a framework that generates pseudo-anomalies directly in the latent space, avoiding handcrafted anomaly injections and the need for domain expertise. A latent-space decoder produces tailored pseudo-anomalies to train a Transformer-based anomaly classifier, while a pre-trained LLM enriches the temporal and contextual representations of this space. Experiments on three benchmark datasets show that ASTER achieves state-of-the-art performance and sets a new standard for LLM-based TSAD.

AI.tldr: ASTER introduces a novel framework for time-series anomaly detection by generating pseudo-anomalies directly in latent space, using a Transformer-based classifier and a pre-trained LLM to enhance representations. It outperforms existing methods across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the limitations of current unsupervised time-series anomaly detection methods, particularly their reliance on reconstruction, fixed distance metrics, and domain-specific anomaly synthesis, which hinder performance on complex data.
AI.method: ASTER operates by generating pseudo-anomalies in latent space through a latent-space decoder. These pseudo-anomalies are used to train a Transformer-based anomaly classifier, while a pre-trained large language model (LLM) enriches temporal and contextual representations.
AI.result: The method demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on three benchmark datasets for time-series anomaly detection, showing its ability to surpass existing methods.
AI.conclusion: ASTER provides an effective, domain-agnostic framework for TSAD by leveraging pseudo-anomalies in latent space, a Transformer-based classifier, and enriched representations via LLM. This sets a new standard for LLM-based TSAD approaches.

title: A Multi-Stage Optimization Pipeline for Bethesda Cell Detection in Pap Smear Cytology
comment: ISBI 2026 Accepted Paper & Second Place Solution for the RIVA Cervical Cytology Challenge Track B
summary: Computer vision techniques have advanced significantly in recent years, finding diverse and impactful applications within the medical field. In this paper, we introduce a new framework for the detection of Bethesda cells in Pap smear images, developed for Track B of the Riva Cytology Challenge held in association with the International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI). This work focuses on enhancing computer vision models for cell detection, with performance evaluated using the mAP50-95 metric. We propose a solution based on an ensemble of YOLO and U-Net architectures, followed by a refinement stage utilizing overlap removal techniques and a binary classifier. Our framework achieved second place with a mAP50-95 score of 0.5909 in the competition. The implementation and source code are available at the following repository: github.com/martinamster/riva-trackb

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a framework to detect Bethesda cells in Pap smear images using an ensemble of YOLO and U-Net models, achieving second place in a biomedical imaging competition with a mAP50-95 score of 0.5909.
AI.motivation: To advance computer vision applications in medical diagnostics, specifically improving the detection of Bethesda cells in Pap smear images for enhanced cytological analysis.
AI.method: The approach employs an ensemble of YOLO and U-Net architectures for cell detection, incorporates overlap removal techniques, and refines predictions using a binary classifier.
AI.result: The framework achieved second place in the Riva Cytology Challenge with a mAP50-95 score of 0.5909, demonstrating its effectiveness and competitiveness.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method showcases the potential of advanced computer vision techniques in medical diagnostics, offering openly accessible implementation and code to encourage further research and development.

title: Unsupervised Anomaly Detection in Process-Complex Industrial Time Series: A Real-World Case Study
summary:
Industrial time-series data from real production environments exhibits substantially higher complexity than commonly used benchmark datasets, primarily due to heterogeneous, multi-stage operational processes. As a result, anomaly detection methods validated under simplified conditions often fail to generalize to industrial settings. This work presents an empirical study on a unique dataset collected from fully operational industrial machinery, explicitly capturing pronounced process-induced variability.
  We evaluate which model classes are capable of capturing this complexity, starting with a classical Isolation Forest baseline and extending to multiple autoencoder architectures. Experimental results show that Isolation Forest is insufficient for modeling the non-periodic, multi-scale dynamics present in the data, whereas autoencoders consistently perform better. Among them, temporal convolutional autoencoders achieve the most robust performance, while recurrent and variational variants require more careful tuning.

AI.tldr: Industrial anomaly detection is challenging due to complex production data. Autoencoders outperform Isolation Forest, with temporal convolutional autoencoders showing the best performance.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to explore anomaly detection methods that can generalize to industrial datasets, which are more complex than benchmark datasets.
AI.method: Empirical study using a dataset from operational industrial machinery. Models like Isolation Forest and various autoencoder architectures are evaluated.
AI.result: Isolation Forest fails to handle the complexity of industrial data, while autoencoder models, especially temporal convolutional autoencoders, offer superior performance.
AI.conclusion: Temporal convolutional autoencoders are particularly effective for industrial anomaly detection, outperforming other methods and requiring less fine-tuning.

title: Young people's perceptions and recommendations for conversational generative artificial intelligence in youth mental health
summary: Conversational generative artificial intelligence agents (or genAI chatbots) could benefit youth mental health, yet young people's perspectives remain underexplored. We examined the Mental health Intelligence Agent (Mia), a genAI chatbot originally designed for professionals in Australian youth services. Following co-design, 32 young people participated in online workshops exploring their perceptions of genAI chatbots in youth mental health and to develop recommendations for reconceptualising Mia for consumers and integrating it into services. Four themes were developed: (1) Humanising AI without dehumanising care, (2) I need to know what's under the hood, (3) Right tool, right place, right time?, and (4) Making it mine on safe ground. This study offers insights into young people's attitudes, needs, and requirements regarding genAI chatbots in youth mental health, with key implications for service integration. Additionally, by co-designing system requirements, this work informs the ethics, design, development, implementation, and governance of genAI chatbots in youth mental health contexts.

AI.tldr: This study explores young people's perspectives on the use of generative AI chatbots for mental health support, focusing on optimizing the design and integration of Mia, a chatbot initially created for professionals.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the underexplored perspectives of young people on genAI chatbots in mental health and adapt the chatbot Mia for broader consumer use.
AI.method: The researchers conducted co-design workshops with 32 young participants to gather insights and recommendations for adapting Mia into youth mental health services.
AI.result: Four themes emerged: humanizing AI while maintaining human care, the need for transparency in AI systems, ensuring the right application context, and creating tailored, safe experiences.
